{{Otheruses|the historical term "Shinden"|other uses of "Kanda"}} {{Cite needed|date=August 2023}} '''Shinden''' (神田, also read ''Kanda'') refers to land (御田) in [[Japan]] designated for covering the expenses of rituals and operations of [[Shinto shrines]]. It is also referred to as '''Mitashiro''' (御戸代), '''Omita''' or '''Onta''' (御神田), and '''Ōmita''' (大御田).
== History == [[File:Jingu-shinden 01.JPG|thumb|right|256px|The sacred rice fields (Shinden) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} [[File:Jingu-sinden-otaue 03.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Rice planting ceremony in the sacred fields of the shrine]] [[File:Yushima-Tenmagu-01.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Sacred rice harvest ceremony at {{ill|Yushima Tenman-gū|ja|湯島天満宮}} The exact origins of Shinden are unclear, but it is believed to have existed before the [[Taika Reform]] (circa 646 CE). Even after the [[Ritsuryō system]] was established in the late 7th century and agricultural land became subject to the {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法}} system, Shinden (and land used for operating Buddhist temples, known as {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}) was exempt from redistribution. This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the [[Taihō Code]] and [[Yōrō Code]] enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)}}) would use the taxes they paid ({{ill|Soyocho system|ja|租庸調}}) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the [[Heian period]]. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include {{ill|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)|ja|神田 (千代田区)}} in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.
== See Also == * {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}} * [[Tsukuda]] * {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)}} * {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法}} * [[Shōen (Japan)|Shōen]] * [[Isehikari]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵== See Also ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|the historical term "Shinden"|other uses of "Kanda"}} {{Cite needed|date=August 2023}} '''Shinden''' (神田, also read ''Kanda'') refers to land (御田) in [[Japan]] designated for covering the expenses of rituals and operations of [[Shinto shrines]]. It is also referred to as '''Mitashiro''' (御戸代), '''Omita''' or '''Onta''' (御神田), and '''Ōmita''' (大御田).
== History == [[File:Jingu-shinden 01.JPG|thumb|right|256px|The sacred rice fields (Shinden) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} [[File:Jingu-sinden-otaue 03.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Rice planting ceremony in the sacred fields of the shrine]] [[File:Yushima-Tenmagu-01.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Sacred rice harvest ceremony at {{ill|Yushima Tenman-gū|ja|湯島天満宮}} The exact origins of Shinden are unclear, but it is believed to have existed before the [[Taika Reform]] (circa 646 CE). Even after the [[Ritsuryō system]] was established in the late 7th century and agricultural land became subject to the {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法}} system, Shinden (and land used for operating Buddhist temples, known as {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}) was exempt from redistribution. This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the [[Taihō Code]] and [[Yōrō Code]] enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)}}) would use the taxes they paid ({{ill|Soyocho system|ja|租庸調}}) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the [[Heian period]]. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include {{ill|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)|ja|神田 (千代田区)}} in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.== See Also == * {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}} * [[Tsukuda]] * {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)}} * {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法}} * [[Shōen (Japan)|Shōen]] * [[Isehikari]]
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/summary_3.html Sacred Fields of Ise Grand Shrine] * [http://shinden.boo.jp/wiki/%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E7%A5%9E%E5%AE%AE%E7%A5%9E%E7%94%B0 Ise Grand Shrine Sacred Fields]

{{Otheruses|the historical term "Shinden"|other uses of "Kanda"}} {{Cite needed|date=August 2023}} '''Shinden''' (神田, also read ''Kanda'') refers to land (御田) in [[Japan]] designated for covering the expenses of rituals and operations of [[Shinto shrines]]. It is also referred to as '''Mitashiro''' (御戸代), '''Omita''' or '''Onta''' (御神田), and '''Ōmita''' (大御田).
== History == [[File:Jingu-shinden 01.JPG|thumb|right|256px|The sacred rice fields (Shinden) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} [[File:Jingu-sinden-otaue 03.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Rice planting ceremony in the sacred fields of the shrine]] [[File:Yushima-Tenmagu-01.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Sacred rice harvest ceremony at {{ill|Yushima Tenman-gū|ja|湯島天満宮}} The exact origins of Shinden are unclear, but it is believed to have existed before the [[Taika Reform]] (circa 646 CE). Even after the [[Ritsuryō system]] was established in the late 7th century and agricultural land became subject to the {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法}} system, Shinden (and land used for operating Buddhist temples, known as {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}) was exempt from redistribution. This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the [[Taihō Code]] and [[Yōrō Code]] enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)}}) would use the taxes they paid ({{ill|Soyocho system|ja|租庸調}}) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the [[Heian period]]. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include {{ill|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)|ja|神田 (千代田区)}} in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden. == See Also! == * {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}} * [[Tsukuda]] * {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)}} * {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法}} * [[Shōen (Japan)|Shōen]] * [[Isehikari]]
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/summary_3.html Sacred Fields of Ise Grand Shrine] * [http://shinden.boo.jp/wiki/%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E7%A5%9E%E5%AE%AE%E7%A5%9E%E7%94%B0 Ise Grand Shrine Sacred Fields]

{{Otheruses|the historical term "Shinden"|other uses of "Kanda"}} {{Cite needed|date=August 2023}} '''Shinden''' (神田, also read ''Kanda'') refers to land (御田) in [[Japan]] designated for covering the expenses of rituals and operations of [[Shinto shrines]]. It is also referred to as '''Mitashiro''' (御戸代), '''Omita''' or '''Onta''' (御神田), and '''Ōmita''' (大御田).
== History == [[File:Jingu-shinden 01.JPG|thumb|right|256px|The sacred rice fields (Shinden) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} [[File:Jingu-sinden-otaue 03.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Rice planting ceremony in the sacred fields of the shrine]] [[File:Yushima-Tenmagu-01.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Sacred rice harvest ceremony at {{ill|Yushima Tenman-gū|ja|湯島天満宮}} The exact origins of Shinden are unclear, but it is believed to have existed before the [[Taika Reform]] (circa 646 CE). Even after the [[Ritsuryō system]] was established in the late 7th century and agricultural land became subject to the {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法}} system, Shinden (and land used for operating Buddhist temples, known as {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}}) was exempt from redistribution. This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the [[Taihō Code]] and [[Yōrō Code]] enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)}}) would use the taxes they paid ({{ill|Soyocho system|ja|租庸調}}) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the [[Heian period]]. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include {{ill|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)|ja|神田 (千代田区)}} in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden. == See Also == * {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田}} * [[Tsukuda]] * {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)}} * {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法}} * [[Shōen (Japan)|Shōen]] * [[Isehikari]]
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/summary_3.html Sacred Fields of Ise Grand Shrine] * [http://shinden.boo.jp/wiki/%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E7%A5%9E%E5%AE%AE%E7%A5%9E%E7%94%B0 Ise Grand Shrine Sacred Fields]

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|the historical term "Shinden"|other uses of "Kanda"}} {{Cite needed|date=August 2023}} '''Shinden''' (神田, also read ''Kanda'') refers to land (御田) in [[Japan]] designated for covering the expenses of rituals and operations of [[Shinto shrines]]. It is also referred to as '''Mitashiro''' (御戸代), '''Omita''' or '''Onta''' (御神田), and '''Ōmita''' (大御田).
== History == [[File:Jingu-shinden 01.JPG|thumb|right|256px|The sacred rice fields (Shinden) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}} [[File:Jingu-sinden-otaue 03.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Rice planting ceremony in the sacred fields of the shrine]] [[File:Yushima-Tenmagu-01.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Sacred rice harvest ceremony at {{ill|Yushima Tenman-gū|ja|湯島天満宮|12=draft}} The exact origins of Shinden are unclear, but it is believed to have existed before the [[Taika Reform]] (circa 646 CE). Even after the [[Ritsuryō system]] was established in the late 7th century and agricultural land became subject to the {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|12=draft}} system, Shinden (and land used for operating Buddhist temples, known as {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=draft}}) was exempt from redistribution. This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the [[Taihō Code]] and [[Yōrō Code]] enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令|12=draft}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=draft}}) would use the taxes they paid ({{ill|Soyocho system|ja|租庸調|12=draft}}) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the [[Heian period]]. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵|12=draft}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主|12=draft}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include {{ill|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)|ja|神田 (千代田区)|12=draft}} in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|12=draft}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden. == See Also == * {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=draft}} * [[Tsukuda]] * {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|12=draft}} * [[Shōen (Japan)|Shōen]] * [[Isehikari]]
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/summary_3.html Sacred Fields of Ise Grand Shrine] * [http://shinden.boo.jp/wiki/%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E7%A5%9E%E5%AE%AE%E7%A5%9E%E7%94%B0 Ise Grand Shrine Sacred Fields]
{{citation needed|Shinden}}

The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden. == See Also == * {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=draft}} * [[Tsukuda]] * {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|12=draft}} * [[Shōen (Japan)|Shōen]] * [[Isehikari]]

{{Otheruses|the historical term "Shinden"|other uses of "Kanda"}} {{Cite needed|date=August 2023}} '''Shinden''' (神田, also read ''Kanda'') refers to land (御田) in [[Japan]] designated for covering the expenses of rituals and operations of [[Shinto shrines]]. It is also referred to as '''Mitashiro''' (御戸代), '''Omita''' or '''Onta''' (御神田), and '''Ōmita''' (大御田).
== History == [[File:Jingu-shinden 01.JPG|thumb|right|256px|The sacred rice fields (Shinden) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} [[File:Jingu-sinden-otaue 03.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Rice planting ceremony in the sacred fields of the shrine]] [[File:Yushima-Tenmagu-01.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Sacred rice harvest ceremony at {{ill|Yushima Tenman-gū|ja|湯島天満宮|12=draft|12=draft}} The exact origins of Shinden are unclear, but it is believed to have existed before the [[Taika Reform]] (circa 646 CE). Even after the [[Ritsuryō system]] was established in the late 7th century and agricultural land became subject to the {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|12=draft|12=draft}} system, Shinden (and land used for operating Buddhist temples, known as {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=draft|12=draft}}) was exempt from redistribution. This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the [[Taihō Code]] and [[Yōrō Code]] enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令|12=draft|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令|12=draft|12=draft}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=draft|12=draft}}) would use the taxes they paid ({{ill|Soyocho system|ja|租庸調|12=draft|12=draft}}) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the [[Heian period]]. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵|12=draft|12=draft}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主|12=draft|12=draft}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include {{ill|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)|ja|神田 (千代田区)|12=draft|12=draft}} in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|12=draft|12=draft}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.

Even after the [[Ritsuryō system]] was established in the late 7th century and agricultural land became subject to the {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|12=draft|12=draft}} system, Shinden (and land used for operating Buddhist temples, known as {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=draft|12=draft}}) was exempt from redistribution. This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the [[Taihō Code]] and [[Yōrō Code]] enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令|12=draft|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令|12=draft|12=draft}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=draft|12=draft}}) would use the taxes they paid ({{ill|Soyocho system|ja|租庸調|12=draft|12=draft}}) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the [[Heian period]]. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵|12=draft|12=draft}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主|12=draft|12=draft}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include {{ill|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)|ja|神田 (千代田区)|12=draft|12=draft}} in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|12=draft|12=draft}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.
== See Also == * {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=draft|12=draft}} * [[Tsukuda]] * {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|12=draft|12=draft}} * [[Shōen (Japan)|Shōen]] * [[Isehikari]]
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/summary_3.html Sacred Fields of Ise Grand Shrine] * [http://shinden.boo.jp/wiki/%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E7%A5%9E%E5%AE%AE%E7%A5%9E%E7%94%B0 Ise Grand Shrine Sacred Fields]
{{citation needed|Shinden}}

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|歴史用語としての神田|その他の神田}} {{出典の明記|date=2023年8月}} '''神田'''（しんでん、かんだ）とは、[[日本]]において、[[神社]]の祭祀などの運営経費にあてる[[御田|領田]]（[[寺社領]]）のことをいう。'''御戸代'''（みとしろ）、'''御神田'''（おみた、おんた）、'''大御田'''（おおみた）とも。
== 沿革 == [[Image:Jingu-shinden 01.JPG|thumb|right|256px|[[伊勢神宮]]の神田]] [[Image:Jingu-sinden-otaue 03.jpg|thumb|right|256px|神宮神田での神田御田植初]] [[Image:Yushima-Tenmagu-01.jpg|thumb|right|256px|[[湯島天満宮]] 御神田初穂]] 神田の起源は明らかとされていないが、[[大化]]（[[646年]]ごろ）以前から存在したと考えられている。[[7世紀]]後半に[[律令制]]が整備され、田地が[[口分田]]などの[[班田収授法|班田収授]]の体系に組み込まれていっても、神田（および仏教寺院の運営にあてる[[寺田]]）のみは、班田の対象外とされた。これは、神田および寺田が、神社や寺院の所有物ではなく、神仏に帰属するものと認識されていたことによる。そのため、神仏に帰属する神田・寺田の売買は禁止されていた。
[[8世紀]]に成立した[[大宝律令]]・[[養老律令]]では、神祇令・田令などに神田の規定が置かれた。それによると、神田を耕作するために、神戸（神に帰属する戸）が設定され、神戸にかかる[[租庸調]]は、神社の造営・運営経費にあてること、そして6年1班の班田収授の対象から除外することが規定されていた。すなわち、神田は[[不輸租田]]（租税が免除された田地）とされていた。
神田を不輸租田とする観念は、[[平安時代]]の[[荘園 (日本)|荘園]]の増加につながっていく。[[9世紀]]～[[10世紀]]に律令制が崩壊した後も、神田には不輸の権（租税免除の権利）が認められていたため、[[墾田]]や買収などで付近の田地を集積していた[[田堵]]（有力農民）＝[[開発領主]]は、自分の経営する田地を有力な神社（または有力寺院）へ寄進することで、[[不輸の権 (日本)|不輸の権]]を獲得しようとした。そのため、有力寺社には荘園の寄進が集中した。
その後、[[11世紀]]から[[13世紀]]ごろに[[荘園公領制]]が成立すると、[[荘園 (日本)|荘園]]や[[国衙領]]の除田（じょでん、免税田を意味する）の一つとして神田が位置づけられた。神田にかかる[[年貢]]・[[公事]]は、領主の収入とはならず、神社の祭祀・祭礼の経費にあてられた。こうした慣行はその後も続き、現代でも多くの神社で神に供御するための田として、神田・御神田が存続している。
== 地名・名字 == 日本の地名としての神田は、上記の神田に由来する。神田は日本各地に存在したため、地名としての神田も日本全国に分布している。著名な神田には、[[神田 (千代田区)|千代田区神田]]、唐津市の[[神田 (唐津市)]]（こうだ）などがある。
日本の名字（姓）の神田も、上記の神田に由来する。 <!-- == 脚注 == <references/>-->
== 関連項目 == * [[寺田]] * [[佃]] * [[神戸 (民戸)]] * [[班田収授法]] * [[荘園 (日本)|荘園]] *[[イセヒカリ]]
==外部リンク== *[http://www.isejingu.or.jp/summary_3.html 神宮神田] *[http://shinden.boo.jp/wiki/%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E7%A5%9E%E5%AE%AE%E7%A5%9E%E7%94%B0 伊勢神宮神田]
{{DEFAULTSORT:しんてん}} [[Category:日本の律令制]] [[Category:日本の荘園制]]

{{Otheruses|歴史用語としての神田|その他の神田}} {{出典の明記|date=2023年8月}} '''神田'''（しんでん、かんだ）とは、[[日本]]において、[[神社]]の祭祀などの運営経費にあてる[[御田|領田]]（[[寺社領]]）のことをいう。'''御戸代'''（みとしろ）、'''御神田'''（おみた、おんた）、'''大御田'''（おおみた）とも。
== 沿革 == [[Image:Jingu-shinden 01.JPG|thumb|right|256px|[[伊勢神宮]]の神田]] [[Image:Jingu-sinden-otaue 03.jpg|thumb|right|256px|神宮神田での神田御田植初]] [[Image:Yushima-Tenmagu-01.jpg|thumb|right|256px|[[湯島天満宮]] 御神田初穂]] 神田の起源は明らかとされていないが、[[大化]]（[[646年]]ごろ）以前から存在したと考えられている。[[7世紀]]後半に[[律令制]]が整備され、田地が[[口分田]]などの[[班田収授法|班田収授]]の体系に組み込まれていっても、神田（および仏教寺院の運営にあてる[[寺田]]）のみは、班田の対象外とされた。これは、神田および寺田が、神社や寺院の所有物ではなく、神仏に帰属するものと認識されていたことによる。そのため、神仏に帰属する神田・寺田の売買は禁止されていた。
[[8世紀]]に成立した[[大宝律令]]・[[養老律令]]では、神祇令・田令などに神田の規定が置かれた。それによると、神田を耕作するために、神戸（神に帰属する戸）が設定され、神戸にかかる[[租庸調]]は、神社の造営・運営経費にあてること、そして6年1班の班田収授の対象から除外することが規定されていた。すなわち、神田は[[不輸租田]]（租税が免除された田地）とされていた。
神田を不輸租田とする観念は、[[平安時代]]の[[荘園 (日本)|荘園]]の増加につながっていく。[[9世紀]]～[[10世紀]]に律令制が崩壊した後も、神田には不輸の権（租税免除の権利）が認められていたため、[[墾田]]や買収などで付近の田地を集積していた[[田堵]]（有力農民）＝[[開発領主]]は、自分の経営する田地を有力な神社（または有力寺院）へ寄進することで、[[不輸の権 (日本)|不輸の権]]を獲得しようとした。そのため、有力寺社には荘園の寄進が集中した。
その後、[[11世紀]]から[[13世紀]]ごろに[[荘園公領制]]が成立すると、[[荘園 (日本)|荘園]]や[[国衙領]]の除田（じょでん、免税田を意味する）の一つとして神田が位置づけられた。神田にかかる[[年貢]]・[[公事]]は、領主の収入とはならず、神社の祭祀・祭礼の経費にあてられた。こうした慣行はその後も続き、現代でも多くの神社で神に供御するための田として、神田・御神田が存続している。
== 地名・名字 == 日本の地名としての神田は、上記の神田に由来する。神田は日本各地に存在したため、地名としての神田も日本全国に分布している。著名な神田には、[[神田 (千代田区)|千代田区神田]]、唐津市の[[神田 (唐津市)]]（こうだ）などがある。
日本の名字（姓）の神田も、上記の神田に由来する。 <!-- == 脚注 == <references/>-->
== 関連項目 == * [[寺田]] * [[佃]] * [[神戸 (民戸)]] * [[班田収授法]] * [[荘園 (日本)|荘園]] *[[イセヒカリ]]
==外部リンク== *[http://www.isejingu.or.jp/summary_3.html 神宮神田] *[http://shinden.boo.jp/wiki/%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E7%A5%9E%E5%AE%AE%E7%A5%9E%E7%94%B0 伊勢神宮神田]
{{DEFAULTSORT:しんてん}} [[Category:日本の律令制]] [[Category:日本の荘園制]]

{{Otheruses|the historical term "Shinden"|other uses of "Kanda"}} {{Cite needed|date=August 2023}} '''Shinden''' (神田, also read ''Kanda'') refers to land (御田) in [[Japan]] designated for covering the expenses of rituals and operations of [[Shinto shrines]]. It is also referred to as '''Mitashiro''' (御戸代), '''Omita''' or '''Onta''' (御神田), and '''Ōmita''' (大御田).
== History == [[File:Jingu-shinden 01.JPG|thumb|right|256px|The sacred rice fields (Shinden) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} [[File:Jingu-sinden-otaue 03.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Rice planting ceremony in the sacred fields of the shrine]] [[File:Yushima-Tenmagu-01.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Sacred rice harvest ceremony at {{ill|Yushima Tenman-gū|ja|湯島天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} The exact origins of Shinden are unclear, but it is believed to have existed before the [[Taika Reform]] (circa 646 CE). Even after the [[Ritsuryō system]] was established in the late 7th century and agricultural land became subject to the {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|qq=draft|qq=draft}} system, Shinden (and land used for operating Buddhist temples, known as {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) was exempt from redistribution. This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the [[Taihō Code]] and [[Yōrō Code]] enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) would use the taxes they paid ({{ill|Soyocho system|ja|租庸調|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the [[Heian period]]. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include {{ill|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)|ja|神田 (千代田区)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.
== See Also == * {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * [[Tsukuda]] * {{ill|Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * [[Shōen (Japan)|Shōen]] * [[Isehikari]]
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/summary_3.html Sacred Fields of Ise Grand Shrine] * [http://shinden.boo.jp/wiki/%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E7%A5%9E%E5%AE%AE%E7%A5%9E%E7%94%B0 Ise Grand Shrine Sacred Fields]
{{translated page|ja|神田|version=96485441|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{Otheruses|the historical term "Shinden"|other uses of "Kanda"}} {{Cite needed|date=August 2023}} '''Shinden''' (神田, also read ''Kanda'') refers to land (御田) in [[Japan]] designated for covering the expenses of rituals and operations of [[Shinto shrines]]. == History == [[File:Jingu-shinden 01.JPG|thumb|right|256px|The sacred rice fields (Shinden) of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] [[File:Jingu-sinden-otaue 03.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Rice planting ceremony in the sacred fields of the shrine]] [[File:Yushima-Tenmagu-01.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Sacred rice harvest ceremony at [[:en:Yushima Tenmangū|Yushima Tenman-gū]] The exact origins of Shinden are unclear, but it is believed to have existed before the [[Taika Reform]] (circa 646 CE). Notable examples include [[:en:Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)]] in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (Karatsu)|qq=}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:日本の律令制
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|the historical term "Shinden"|other uses of "Kanda"}} {{Cite needed|date=August 2023}} '''Shinden''' (神田, also read ''Kanda'') refers to land (御田) in [[Japan]] designated for covering the expenses of rituals and operations of [[Shinto shrines]]. It is also referred to as '''Mitashiro''' (御戸代), '''Omita''' or '''Onta''' (御神田), and '''Ōmita''' (大御田).
== History == [[File:Jingu-shinden 01.JPG|thumb|right|256px|The sacred rice fields (Shinden) of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] [[File:Jingu-sinden-otaue 03.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Rice planting ceremony in the sacred fields of the shrine]] [[File:Yushima-Tenmagu-01.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Sacred rice harvest ceremony at [[:en:Yushima Tenmangū|Yushima Tenman-gū]] The exact origins of Shinden are unclear, but it is believed to have existed before the [[Taika Reform]] (circa 646 CE). Even after the [[Ritsuryō system]] was established in the late 7th century and agricultural land became subject to the {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|qq=}} system, Shinden (and land used for operating Buddhist temples, known as {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}) was exempt from redistribution. This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the [[Taihō Code]] and [[Yōrō Code]] enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingi Code|qq=}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Denryō|qq=}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}}) would use the taxes they paid ([[:en:Zu Yong Diao|Soyocho system]]) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the [[Heian period]]. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tato|qq=}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaihatsu Ryōshu|qq=}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include [[:en:Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)]] in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (Karatsu)|qq=}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.
== See Also == * {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}} * [[Tsukuda]] * {{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}} * {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|qq=}} * [[Shōen (Japan)|Shōen]] * [[Isehikari]]
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/summary_3.html Sacred Fields of Ise Grand Shrine] * [http://shinden.boo.jp/wiki/%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E7%A5%9E%E5%AE%AE%E7%A5%9E%E7%94%B0 Ise Grand Shrine Sacred Fields]
{{translated page|ja|神田|version=96485441|comment=Imported full ja history}}

== History == [[File:Jingu-shinden 01.JPG|thumb|right|256px|The sacred rice fields (Shinden) of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] [[File:Jingu-sinden-otaue 03.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Rice planting ceremony in the sacred fields of the shrine]] [[File:Yushima-Tenmagu-01.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Sacred rice harvest ceremony at [[:en:Yushima Tenmangū|Yushima Tenman-gū]] The exact origins of Shinden are unclear, but it is believed to have existed before the [[Taika Reform]] (circa 646 CE). Even after the [[Ritsuryō system]] was established in the late 7th century and agricultural land became subject to the {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|qq=}} system, Shinden (and land used for operating Buddhist temples, known as {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}) was exempt from redistribution. This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the [[Taihō Code]] and [[Yōrō Code]] enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingi Code|qq=}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Denryō|qq=}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}}) would use the taxes they paid ([[:en:Zu Yong Diao|Soyocho system]]) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the [[Heian period]]. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tato|qq=}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaihatsu Ryōshu|qq=}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include [[:en:Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)]] in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (Karatsu)|qq=}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.
== See Also == * {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}} * [[Tsukuda]] * {{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}} * {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|qq=}} * [[Shōen (Japan)|Shōen]] * [[Isehikari]]
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/summary_3.html Sacred Fields of Ise Grand Shrine] * [http://shinden.boo.jp/wiki/%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E7%A5%9E%E5%AE%AE%E7%A5%9E%E7%94%B0 Ise Grand Shrine Sacred Fields]

Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.

Notable examples include [[:en:Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)]] in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (Karatsu)|qq=}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|the historical term "Shinden"|other uses of "Kanda"}} {{Unreferenceded|date=August 2023}} '''Shinden''' (神田, also read ''Kanda'') refers to land (御田) in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] designated for covering the expenses of rituals and operations of [[Shinto shrines]]. It is also referred to as '''Mitashiro''' (御戸代), '''Omita''' or '''Onta''' (御神田), and '''Ōmita''' (大御田).
== History == [[File:Jingu-shinden 01.JPG|thumb|right|256px|The sacred rice fields (Shinden) of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] [[File:Jingu-sinden-otaue 03.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Rice planting ceremony in the sacred fields of the shrine]] [[File:Yushima-Tenmagu-01.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Sacred rice harvest ceremony at [[:en:Yushima Tenmangū|Yushima Tenman-gū]] The exact origins of Shinden are unclear, but it is believed to have existed before the [[Taika Reform]] (circa 646 CE). Even after the [[Ritsuryō system]] was established in the late 7th century and agricultural land became subject to the {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|qq=}} system, Shinden (and land used for operating Buddhist temples, known as {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}) was exempt from redistribution. This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the [[Taihō Code]] and [[Yōrō Code]] enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingi Code|qq=}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Denryō|qq=}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}}) would use the taxes they paid ([[:en:Zu Yong Diao|Soyocho system]]) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tato|qq=}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaihatsu Ryōshu|qq=}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include [[:en:Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)]] in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (Karatsu)|qq=}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.
== See Also == * {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}} * [[Tsukuda]] * {{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}} * {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|qq=}} * [[Shōen (Japan)|Shōen]] * [[Isehikari]]

{{Otheruses|the historical term "Shinden"|other uses of "Kanda"}} {{Unreferenceded|date=August 2023}} '''Shinden''' (神田, also read ''Kanda'') refers to land (御田) in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] designated for covering the expenses of rituals and operations of [[Shinto shrines]]. It is also referred to as '''Mitashiro''' (御戸代), '''Omita''' or '''Onta''' (御神田), and '''Ōmita''' (大御田).
== History == [[File:Jingu-shinden 01.JPG|thumb|right|256px|The sacred rice fields (Shinden) of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] [[File:Jingu-sinden-otaue 03.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Rice planting ceremony in the sacred fields of the shrine]] [[File:Yushima-Tenmagu-01.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Sacred rice harvest ceremony at [[:en:Yushima Tenmangū|Yushima Tenman-gū]] The exact origins of Shinden are unclear, but it is believed to have existed before the [[Taika Reform]] (circa 646 CE). Even after the [[Ritsuryō system]] was established in the late 7th century and agricultural land became subject to the {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|qq=}} system, Shinden (and land used for operating Buddhist temples, known as {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}}) was exempt from redistribution. This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingi Code|qq=}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Denryō|qq=}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}}) would use the taxes they paid ([[:en:Zu Yong Diao|Soyocho system]]) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tato|qq=}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaihatsu Ryōshu|qq=}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include [[:en:Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)]] in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (Karatsu)|qq=}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.
== See Also == * {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=}} * [[Tsukuda]] * {{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}} * {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|qq=}} * [[Shōen (Japan)|Shōen]] * [[Isehikari]]
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/summary_3.html Sacred Fields of Ise Grand Shrine] * [http://shinden.boo.jp/wiki/%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E7%A5%9E%E5%AE%AE%E7%A5%9E%E7%94%B0 Ise Grand Shrine Sacred Fields]
{{translated page|ja|神田|version=96485441|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10855080]] Wikidata name: shinden [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|the historical term "Shinden"|other uses of "Kanda"}} {{Unreferenceded|date=August 2023}} '''Shinden''' (神田, also read ''Kanda'') refers to land (御田) in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] designated for covering the expenses of rituals and operations of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingi Code|qq=}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Denryō|qq=}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}}) would use the taxes they paid ([[:en:Zu Yong Diao|Soyocho system]]) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tato|qq=}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaihatsu Ryōshu|qq=}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include [[:en:Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)]] in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (Karatsu)|qq=}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.

This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingi Code|qq=}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Denryō|qq=}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}}) would use the taxes they paid ([[:en:Zu Yong Diao|Soyocho system]]) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tato|qq=|1=tato|comment=changed title based on wikidata}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaihatsu Ryōshu|qq=|1=kaihotsu ryōshu|comment=changed title based on wikidata}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include [[:en:Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)]] in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (Karatsu)|qq=}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.

revision.comment: Bot: remove invalid enwiki interwiki links
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10855080]] Wikidata name: shinden [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10855080]] Wikidata name: shinden [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|the historical term "Shinden"|other uses of "Kanda"}} {{Unreferenceded|date=August 2023}} '''Shinden''' (神田, also read ''Kanda'') refers to land (御田) in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] designated for covering the expenses of rituals and operations of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. It is also referred to as '''Mitashiro''' (御戸代), '''Omita''' or '''Onta''' (御神田), and '''Ōmita''' (大御田).
== History == [[File:Jingu-shinden 01.JPG|thumb|right|256px|The sacred rice fields (Shinden) of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] [[File:Jingu-sinden-otaue 03.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Rice planting ceremony in the sacred fields of the shrine]] [[File:Yushima-Tenmagu-01.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Sacred rice harvest ceremony at [[:en:Yushima Tenmangū|Yushima Tenman-gū]] The exact origins of Shinden are unclear, but it is believed to have existed before the [[Taika Reform]] (circa 646 CE). Even after the [[Ritsuryō system]] was established in the late 7th century and agricultural land became subject to the {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|qq=|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} system, Shinden (and land used for operating Buddhist temples, known as {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}}) was exempt from redistribution. This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingi Code|qq=}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Denryō|qq=|1=Denryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65273862}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}}) would use the taxes they paid ([[:en:Zu Yong Diao|Soyocho system]]) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tato|qq=|1=tato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576460}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaihatsu Ryōshu|qq=|1=kaihotsu ryōshu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655292}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include [[:en:Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)]] in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (Karatsu)|qq=}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.
== See Also == * {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}} * [[:en:Tsukuda|Tsukuda]] * {{ill|Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko (Shinto Shrine Fiefs)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}} * {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|qq=|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} * [[Shōen (Japan)|Shōen]] * [[Isehikari]]
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/summary_3.html Sacred Fields of Ise Grand Shrine] * [http://shinden.boo.jp/wiki/%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E7%A5%9E%E5%AE%AE%E7%A5%9E%E7%94%B0 Ise Grand Shrine Sacred Fields]
{{translated page|ja|神田|version=96485441|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include [[:en:Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)]] in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (Karatsu)|qq=}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神田]][[ko:간다 (신토)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10855080]] Wikidata name: shinden [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10855080]] Wikidata name: shinden [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|the historical term "Shinden"|other uses of "Kanda"}} {{Unreferenceded|date=August 2023}} '''Shinden''' (神田, also read ''Kanda'') refers to land (御田) in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] designated for covering the expenses of rituals and operations of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. It is also referred to as '''Mitashiro''' (御戸代), '''Omita''' or '''Onta''' (御神田), and '''Ōmita''' (大御田).
== History == [[File:Jingu-shinden 01.JPG|thumb|right|256px|The sacred rice fields (Shinden) of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] [[File:Jingu-sinden-otaue 03.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Rice planting ceremony in the sacred fields of the shrine]] [[File:Yushima-Tenmagu-01.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Sacred rice harvest ceremony at [[:en:Yushima Tenmangū|Yushima Tenman-gū]] The exact origins of Shinden are unclear, but it is believed to have existed before the [[Taika Reform]] (circa 646 CE). Even after the [[Ritsuryō system]] was established in the late 7th century and agricultural land became subject to the {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|qq=|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} system, Shinden (and land used for operating Buddhist temples, known as {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}}) was exempt from redistribution. This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingi Code|qq=}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Denryō|qq=|1=Denryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65273862}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}}) would use the taxes they paid ([[:en:Zu Yong Diao|Soyocho system]]) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tato|qq=|1=tato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576460}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaihatsu Ryōshu|qq=|1=kaihotsu ryōshu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655292}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include [[:en:Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)]] in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (Karatsu)|qq=}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.
== See Also == * {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|qq=|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}} * [[:en:Tsukuda|Tsukuda]] * {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}} * {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|qq=|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} * [[Shōen (Japan)|Shōen]] * [[Isehikari]]

This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.

These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}}) would use the taxes they paid ([[:en:Zu Yong Diao|Soyocho system]]) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tato|1=tato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576460}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaihatsu Ryōshu|1=kaihotsu ryōshu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655292}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include [[:en:Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)]] in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (Karatsu)|qq=}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.

Even after the [[Ritsuryō system]] was established in the late 7th century and agricultural land became subject to the {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} system, Shinden (and land used for operating Buddhist temples, known as {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}}) was exempt from redistribution. This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingi Code|qq=}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Denryō|1=Denryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65273862}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}}) would use the taxes they paid ([[:en:Zu Yong Diao|Soyocho system]]) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tato|1=tato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576460}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaihatsu Ryōshu|1=kaihotsu ryōshu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655292}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include [[:en:Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)]] in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (Karatsu)|qq=}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神田]][[ko:간다 (신토)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855080]] Wikidata name: shinden [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855080]] Wikidata name: shinden [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|the historical term "Shinden"|other uses of "Kanda"}} {{Unreferenceded|date=August 2023}} '''Shinden''' (神田, also read ''Kanda'') refers to land (御田) in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] designated for covering the expenses of rituals and operations of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. It is also referred to as '''Mitashiro''' (御戸代), '''Omita''' or '''Onta''' (御神田), and '''Ōmita''' (大御田).
== History == [[File:Jingu-shinden 01.JPG|thumb|right|256px|The sacred rice fields (Shinden) of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] [[File:Jingu-sinden-otaue 03.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Rice planting ceremony in the sacred fields of the shrine]] [[File:Yushima-Tenmagu-01.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Sacred rice harvest ceremony at [[:en:Yushima Tenmangū|Yushima Tenman-gū]] The exact origins of Shinden are unclear, but it is believed to have existed before the [[Taika Reform]] (circa 646 CE). Even after the [[Ritsuryō system]] was established in the late 7th century and agricultural land became subject to the {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} system, Shinden (and land used for operating Buddhist temples, known as {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}}) was exempt from redistribution. This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingi Code|qq=}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Denryō|1=Denryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65273862}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}}) would use the taxes they paid ([[:en:Zu Yong Diao|Soyocho system]]) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tato|1=tato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576460}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaihatsu Ryōshu|1=kaihotsu ryōshu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655292}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include [[:en:Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)]] in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (Karatsu)|qq=}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.
== See Also == * {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}} * [[:en:Tsukuda|Tsukuda]] * {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}} * {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} * [[Shōen (Japan)|Shōen]] * [[Isehikari]]
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/summary_3.html Sacred Fields of Ise Grand Shrine] * [http://shinden.boo.jp/wiki/%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E7%A5%9E%E5%AE%AE%E7%A5%9E%E7%94%B0 Ise Grand Shrine Sacred Fields]
{{translated page|ja|神田|version=96485441|comment=Imported full ja history}}

These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}}) would use the taxes they paid ([[:en:Zu Yong Diao|Soyocho system]]) for the construction and operation of shrines.
