From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include [[:en:Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)]] in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (Karatsu)|qq=}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.
== See Also == * {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}} * [[:en:Tsukuda|Tsukuda]] * {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}} * {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} * [[Shōen (Japan)|Shōen]] * [[Isehikari]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神田]][[ko:간다 (신토)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神田]][[ko:간다 (신토)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855080]] Wikidata name: shinden [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855080]] Wikidata name: shinden [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|the historical term "Shinden"|other uses of "Kanda"}} {{Unreferenceded|date=August 2023}} '''Shinden''' (神田, also read ''Kanda'') refers to land (御田) in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] designated for covering the expenses of rituals and operations of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. It is also referred to as '''Mitashiro''' (御戸代), '''Omita''' or '''Onta''' (御神田), and '''Ōmita''' (大御田).
== History == [[File:Jingu-shinden 01.JPG|thumb|right|256px|The sacred rice fields (Shinden) of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] [[File:Jingu-sinden-otaue 03.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Rice planting ceremony in the sacred fields of the shrine]] [[File:Yushima-Tenmagu-01.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Sacred rice harvest ceremony at [[:en:Yushima Tenmangū|Yushima Tenman-gū]] The exact origins of Shinden are unclear, but it is believed to have existed before the [[Taika Reform]] (circa 646 CE). Even after the [[Ritsuryō system]] was established in the late 7th century and agricultural land became subject to the {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} system, Shinden (and land used for operating Buddhist temples, known as {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}}) was exempt from redistribution. This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] and [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingi Code|qq=}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Denryō|1=Denryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65273862}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}}) would use the taxes they paid ([[:en:Zu Yong Diao|Soyocho system]]) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tato|1=tato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576460}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaihatsu Ryōshu|1=kaihotsu ryōshu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655292}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include [[:en:Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)]] in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (Karatsu)|qq=}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.

This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.

These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}}) would use the taxes they paid ([[:en:Zu Yong Diao|Soyocho system]]) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tato|1=tato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576460}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaihatsu Ryōshu|1=kaihotsu ryōshu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655292}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include [[:en:Kanda, Tokyo|Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)]] in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (Karatsu)|qq=}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.
== See Also == * {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}} * [[:en:Tsukuda|Tsukuda]] * {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}} * {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} * [[Shōen (Japan)|Shōen]] * [[Isehikari]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神田]][[ko:간다 (신토)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神田]][[ko:간다 (신토)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神田]][[ko:간다 (신토)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855080]] Wikidata name: shinden [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10855080]] Wikidata name: shinden [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|the historical term "Shinden"|other uses of "Kanda"}} {{Unreferenceded|date=August 2023}} '''Shinden''' (神田, also read ''Kanda'') refers to land (御田) in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} designated for covering the expenses of rituals and operations of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}. It is also referred to as '''Mitashiro''' (御戸代), '''Omita''' or '''Onta''' (御神田), and '''Ōmita''' (大御田).
== History == [[File:Jingu-shinden 01.JPG|thumb|right|256px|The sacred rice fields (Shinden) of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}} [[File:Jingu-sinden-otaue 03.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Rice planting ceremony in the sacred fields of the shrine]] [[File:Yushima-Tenmagu-01.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Sacred rice harvest ceremony at {{ill|Yushima Tenmangū|en|Yushima Tenmangū|WD=Q335606|lt=Yushima Tenman-gū}} The exact origins of Shinden are unclear, but it is believed to have existed before the [[Taika Reform]] (circa 646 CE). Even after the [[Ritsuryō system]] was established in the late 7th century and agricultural land became subject to the {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} system, Shinden (and land used for operating Buddhist temples, known as {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}}) was exempt from redistribution. This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code}} enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingi Code|qq=}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Denryō|1=Denryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65273862}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}}) would use the taxes they paid ({{ill|Zu Yong Diao|en|Zu Yong Diao|WD=Q839365|lt=Soyocho system}}) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tato|1=tato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576460}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaihatsu Ryōshu|1=kaihotsu ryōshu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655292}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include {{ill|Kanda, Tokyo|en|Kanda, Tokyo|WD=Q338861|lt=Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)}} in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (Karatsu)|qq=}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.
== See Also == * {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}} * {{ill|Tsukuda|en|Tsukuda|WD=Q16889009|lt=Tsukuda}} * {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}} * {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} * [[Shōen (Japan)|Shōen]] * [[Isehikari]]
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/summary_3.html Sacred Fields of Ise Grand Shrine] * [http://shinden.boo.jp/wiki/%E4%BC%8A%E5%8B%A2%E7%A5%9E%E5%AE%AE%E7%A5%9E%E7%94%B0 Ise Grand Shrine Sacred Fields]
{{translated page|ja|神田|version=96485441|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:神田]]

These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}}) would use the taxes they paid ({{ill|Zu Yong Diao|en|Zu Yong Diao|WD=Q839365|lt=Soyocho system}}) for the construction and operation of shrines. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include {{ill|Kanda, Tokyo|en|Kanda, Tokyo|WD=Q338861|lt=Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)}} in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (Karatsu)|qq=}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神田]][[ko:간다 (신토)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神田]][[ko:간다 (신토)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神田]][[ko:간다 (신토)]]
{{Otheruses|the historical term "Shinden"|other uses of "Kanda"}} {{Unreferenceded|date=August 2023}} '''Shinden''' (神田, also read ''Kanda'') refers to land (御田) in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} designated for covering the expenses of rituals and operations of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}. It is also referred to as '''Mitashiro''' (御戸代), '''Omita''' or '''Onta''' (御神田), and '''Ōmita''' (大御田).
== History == [[File:Jingu-shinden 01.JPG|thumb|right|256px|The sacred rice fields (Shinden) of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}]] [[File:Jingu-sinden-otaue 03.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Rice planting ceremony in the sacred fields of the shrine]] [[File:Yushima-Tenmagu-01.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Sacred rice harvest ceremony at {{ill|Yushima Tenmangū|en|Yushima Tenmangū|WD=Q335606|lt=Yushima Tenman-gū}}]] The exact origins of Shinden are unclear, but it is believed to have existed before the [[Taika Reform]] (circa 646 CE). Even after the [[Ritsuryō system]] was established in the late 7th century and agricultural land became subject to the {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} system, Shinden (and land used for operating Buddhist temples, known as {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}}) was exempt from redistribution. This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code}} enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingi Code|qq=}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Denryō|1=Denryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65273862}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}}) would use the taxes they paid ({{ill|Zu Yong Diao|en|Zu Yong Diao|WD=Q839365|lt=Soyocho system}}) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tato|1=tato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576460}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaihatsu Ryōshu|1=kaihotsu ryōshu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655292}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include {{ill|Kanda, Tokyo|en|Kanda, Tokyo|WD=Q338861|lt=Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)}} in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (Karatsu)|qq=}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.

Even after the [[Ritsuryō system]] was established in the late 7th century and agricultural land became subject to the {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} system, Shinden (and land used for operating Buddhist temples, known as {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}}) was exempt from redistribution. This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Code}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code|1=Yōrō Code}} enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingi Code|qq=}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Denryō|1=Denryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65273862}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}}) would use the taxes they paid ({{ill|Zu Yong Diao|en|Zu Yong Diao|WD=Q839365|lt=Soyocho system|1=Zu Yong Diao}}) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tato|1=tato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576460}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaihatsu Ryōshu|1=kaihotsu ryōshu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655292}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include {{ill|Kanda, Tokyo|en|Kanda, Tokyo|WD=Q338861|lt=Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)|1=Kanda, Tokyo}} in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (Karatsu)|qq=}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.

{{Otheruses|the historical term "Shinden"|other uses of "Kanda"}} {{Unreferenceded|date=August 2023}} '''Shinden''' (神田, also read ''Kanda'') refers to land (御田) in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} designated for covering the expenses of rituals and operations of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}. It is also referred to as '''Mitashiro''' (御戸代), '''Omita''' or '''Onta''' (御神田), and '''Ōmita''' (大御田).
== History == [[File:Jingu-shinden 01.JPG|thumb|right|256px|The sacred rice fields (Shinden) of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}]] [[File:Jingu-sinden-otaue 03.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Rice planting ceremony in the sacred fields of the shrine]] [[File:Yushima-Tenmagu-01.jpg|thumb|right|256px|Sacred rice harvest ceremony at {{ill|Yushima Tenmangū|en|Yushima Tenmangū|WD=Q335606|lt=Yushima Tenman-gū|1=Yushima Tenmangū}}]] The exact origins of Shinden are unclear, but it is believed to have existed before the [[Taika Reform]] (circa 646 CE). Even after the [[Ritsuryō system]] was established in the late 7th century and agricultural land became subject to the {{ill|Handen-Shūju|ja|班田収授法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handen-Shūju|1=Handen-Shūju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8192257}} system, Shinden (and land used for operating Buddhist temples, known as {{ill|Terada|ja|寺田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Terada|1=Terada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11457954}}) was exempt from redistribution. This exemption was based on the belief that Shinden and Terada belonged to the gods and Buddhas rather than human institutions, thus prohibiting their sale.
Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Code}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code|1=Yōrō Code}} enacted in the 8th century, regulations regarding Shinden were codified in the {{ill|Shingi Code|ja|神祇令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingi Code|qq=}} and the {{ill|Denryō|ja|田令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Denryō|1=Denryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65273862}} sections. These regulations specified that households assigned to cultivate Shinden (referred to as {{ill|Kanbe (social class)|lt=Kanbe (social class)|lt=Jinko|ja|神戸 (民戸)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinko|qq=}}) would use the taxes they paid ({{ill|Zu Yong Diao|en|Zu Yong Diao|WD=Q839365|lt=Soyocho system|1=Zu Yong Diao}}) for the construction and operation of shrines. Furthermore, Shinden was exempt from the six-year redistribution cycle of agricultural land. In essence, Shinden was designated as [[fuyuso-den]] (免租田), or tax-exempt land.
The concept of Shinden as tax-exempt land contributed to the growth of the [[Shōen system|Shōen]] (manorial system) in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. Even after the collapse of the Ritsuryō system in the 9th and 10th centuries, Shinden retained its tax-exempt status. Wealthy farmers known as {{ill|Tato|ja|田堵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tato|1=tato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11576460}} (local landowners) and {{ill|Kaihatsu Ryōshu|ja|開発領主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaihatsu Ryōshu|1=kaihotsu ryōshu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655292}} (developer-lords) sought to have their lands exempted from taxes by donating them to powerful shrines or temples. Consequently, these religious institutions accumulated large amounts of land.
From the 11th to the 13th centuries, during the establishment of the [[Shōen Kōryōsei|Shōen-Public Lands System]], Shinden was classified as one of the exempt lands (除田) within both Shōen and public lands. Taxes and labor obligations (年貢 and 公事) on Shinden were allocated exclusively to shrine operations and rituals rather than to the income of local lords. This tradition continued over time, and even today, many shrines maintain sacred fields for offerings, preserving the legacy of Shinden.
== Place Names and Surnames == Place names derived from Shinden are found throughout Japan, as Shinden existed in many areas. Notable examples include {{ill|Kanda, Tokyo|en|Kanda, Tokyo|WD=Q338861|lt=Kanda (Chiyoda, Tokyo)|1=Kanda, Tokyo}} in Tokyo and {{ill|Kanda (Karatsu)|ja|神田 (唐津市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda (Karatsu)|qq=}} (read as ''Kōda'') in Karatsu City.
The surname Kanda, common in Japan, also originates from the historical use of Shinden.
